title: Credential Reuse Across Multiple Newly-Created Account Registrations
narrative: Fraudster uses a single compromised card to fund multiple newly-created accounts across different platforms or services, exploiting credential validity while distributing activity to avoid per-account velocity flags. Each transaction appears legitimate in isolation—amounts are reasonable, email formats valid, and individual purchase patterns clean. The fraud surface emerges in card-level aggregations: C14 shows the card attached to numerous distinct email addresses within a short window, while C11 reveals the card funding many separate accounts. D1 often equals zero, indicating first-time device-card pairings for each new account setup. The cardholder's legitimate usage pattern does not support simultaneous multi-account creation, but individual transactions pass basic fraud checks because no single account exhibits suspicious velocity or amount behavior.
indicators: C14 > 7 (p90), card linked to many distinct email addresses in rolling window
C11 > 24 (p90), elevated count of unique card-recipient contexts
D1 = 0, first-time pairing of device with this card number
C1 > 60 (p90), card-level transaction counter far exceeds typical cardholder baseline
card1_distinct_addr1 > 4, card used with multiple different billing addresses
TransactionAmt between 50 and 200, per-transaction amount within normal spending range
C8 > 24 (p90), high card-level address diversity counter
indicator_explanations: The card has been associated with an unusually high number of different email accounts recently
Card activity shows connections to many separate recipient or account contexts
This device has never processed a transaction with this card before
Card-level activity volume is far above what the cardholder's history would support
The same card credentials appear with multiple different billing address entries
Individual transaction amounts look reasonable and fit typical purchase patterns
Card shows elevated diversity in address usage across recent transaction history
summary: A compromised card is used to create and fund multiple new accounts across different services, with each transaction appearing clean individually but card-level counters revealing impossible diversity of email addresses, billing contexts, and device pairings.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders rarely create multiple new accounts simultaneously across different platforms. The combination of first-time device associations, elevated email-address diversity, and high card-level context counters—while individual transactions remain behaviorally normal—indicates systematic credential reuse for account creation fraud.
